input.query_abstract: We review theoretical approaches to the understanding of food webs. After an overview of the available food web data, we discuss three different classes of models. The first class comprise static models, which assign links between species according to some simple rule. The second class are dynamical models, which include the population dynamics of several interacting species. We focus on the question of the stability of such webs. The third class are species assembly models and evolutionary models, which build webs starting from a few species by adding new species through a process of "invasion" (assembly models) or "speciation" (evolutionary models). Evolutionary models are found to be capable of building large stable webs.
input.hyp_generic_object: directed chemical reaction networks among molecular compounds
input.hyp_abstract: Theoretical models of chemical reaction networks are reviewed, with compounds as nodes and directed reaction transformations as links. Following a survey of curated reaction databases and mass-spectrometric inventories of complex mixtures, we distinguish three model classes. The first class are static combinatorial models, which connect reactants and products by simple rule-based schemes, such as bond-formation grammars, and characterize the resulting network topology. The second class are dynamical models, in which coupled nonlinear rate equations govern the concentrations of interacting compounds; we focus on the stability of stationary states, deriving conditions under which a steady concentration profile is asymptotically stable from the Jacobian of the mass-action kinetics, and noting that more highly connected reaction systems are not generically stable. The third class are constructive models, in which the network is grown from a small seed set by iteratively introducing new compounds via catalyzed transformations of those already present, retaining only additions that remain producible and non-depleting under the ambient flux. We show that such closure-driven growth can assemble large, self-maintaining autocatalytic organizations that persist far from equilibrium, reproducing the sparse, hierarchical connectivity of complex reaction mixtures, whereas randomly assembled networks of comparable size typically collapse to trivial steady states. We discuss implications for the emergence of self-sustaining chemistry from simple precursor pools.
input.candidate_title: Theory of Rumour Spreading in Complex Social Networks
input.candidate_abstract: We introduce a general stochastic model for the spread of rumours, and derive mean-field equations that describe the dynamics of the model on complex social networks (in particular those mediated by the Internet). We use analytical and numerical solutions of these equations to examine the threshold behavior and dynamics of the model on several models of such networks: random graphs, uncorrelated scale-free networks and scale-free networks with assortative degree correlations. We show that in both homogeneous networks and random graphs the model exhibits a critical threshold in the rumour spreading rate below which a rumour cannot propagate in the system. In the case of scale-free networks, on the other hand, this threshold becomes vanishingly small in the limit of infinite system size. We find that the initial rate at which a rumour spreads is much higher in scale-free networks than in random graphs, and that the rate at which the spreading proceeds on scale-free networks is further increased when assortative degree correlations are introduced. The impact of degree correlations on the final fraction of nodes that ever hears a rumour, however, depends on the interplay between network topology and the rumour spreading rate. Our results show that scale-free social networks are prone to the spreading of rumours, just as they are to the spreading of infections. They are relevant to the spreading dynamics of chain emails, viral advertising and large-scale information dissemination algorithms on the Internet.
